description: Urban places, buildings, and city vocabulary

items.notes: Pronounced 'KAU-pun-ki'. Partitive: kaupunkia. Helsinki is Finland's largest kaupunki.
examples: Helsinki on iso kaupunki. — Helsinki is a big city.
Asun kaupungissa. — I live in the city.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KY-lä'. Note the front vowels y and ä. Partitive: kylää.
examples: Kylä on pieni. — The village is small.
Asuin ennen pienessä kylässä. — I used to live in a small village.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KA-tu'. Partitive: katua. Very common in street names: Mannerheiminkatu.
examples: Katu on leveä. — The street is wide.
Kävelen kadulla. — I'm walking on the street.

items.notes: Pronounced 'TIE' (like 'tee-eh'). The 'ie' is a diphthong. Also means 'way/path'. Partitive: tietä.
examples: Tie on pitkä. — The road is long.
Missä on tie Helsinkiin? — Where is the road to Helsinki?

items.notes: Pronounced 'SIL-ta'. Partitive: siltaa. Finland has many bridges due to its lakes and rivers.
examples: Silta ylittää joen. — The bridge crosses the river.
Kävelen sillan yli. — I walk over the bridge.

items.notes: Pronounced 'PUIS-to'. The 'ui' is a diphthong. Partitive: puistoa.
examples: Puistossa on kaunista. — It's beautiful in the park.
Lapset leikkivät puistossa. — The children play in the park.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KIR-jas-to'. From 'kirja' (book) + -sto suffix. Finnish libraries are famous and well-used. Partitive: kirjastoa.
examples: Kirjasto on auki. — The library is open.
Lainaan kirjan kirjastosta. — I borrow a book from the library.

items.notes: Pronounced 'MU-se-o'. Similar to English. Partitive: museota.
examples: Museo on mielenkiintoinen. — The museum is interesting.
Käymme museossa. — We visit the museum.

items.notes: Pronounced 'TE-at-te-ri'. Double 't' is long. A loanword. Partitive: teatteria.
examples: Menemme teatteriin illalla. — We're going to the theater in the evening.
Teatteri on keskustassa. — The theater is in the center.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KIRK-ko'. Double 'k' is long. Partitive: kirkkoa. The Lutheran church is dominant in Finland.
examples: Kirkko on vanha. — The church is old.
Tuomiokirkko on Helsingin keskustassa. — The cathedral is in the center of Helsinki.

items.notes: Pronounced 'TOR-ni'. Partitive: tornia. 'Näkötorni' = observation tower.
examples: Torni on korkea. — The tower is tall.
Näkötornista näkee kauas. — You can see far from the observation tower.

items.notes: Pronounced 'TO-ri'. Partitive: toria. Finnish cities typically have a tori as the central meeting place. Kauppatori = market square.
examples: Tori on täynnä ihmisiä. — The market square is full of people.
Ostin marjoja torilta. — I bought berries from the market.

items.notes: Pronounced 'PANK-ki'. Double 'k' is long. A loanword from Swedish/German. Partitive: pankkia.
examples: Pankki on kiinni. — The bank is closed.
Menen pankkiin huomenna. — I'm going to the bank tomorrow.

items.notes: Pronounced 'POS-ti'. Means both 'mail' and 'post office'. Partitive: postia.
examples: Vien paketin postiin. — I take the package to the post office.
Onko postia tullut? — Has any mail arrived?

items.notes: Pronounced 'PO-lii-si-A-se-ma'. Compound: poliisi (police) + asema (station). Long double 'i' in poliisi.
examples: Poliisiasema on lähellä. — The police station is nearby.
Menin poliisiasemalle tekemään ilmoituksen. — I went to the police station to file a report.

items.notes: Pronounced 'PA-lo-kun-ta'. Compound: palo (fire) + kunta (department/unit). Partitive: palokuntaa.
examples: Palokunta tuli nopeasti. — The fire department came quickly.
Soita palokunnalle! — Call the fire department!

items.notes: Pronounced 'KOU-lu'. The 'ou' diphthong. Partitive: koulua. Finnish schools are world-famous for quality education.
examples: Koulu alkaa kahdeksalta. — School starts at eight.
Lapset menevät kouluun. — The children go to school.

items.notes: Pronounced 'Y-li-O-pis-to'. Note: starts with 'y' which is a front vowel in Finnish (like German ü). Partitive: yliopistoa.
examples: Opiskelen yliopistossa. — I study at a university.
Helsingin yliopisto on vanha. — The University of Helsinki is old.

items.notes: Pronounced 'LEN-to-kent-tä'. Compound: lento (flight) + kenttä (field). Double 't' is long. Partitive: lentokenttää.
examples: Lentokenttä on kaukana. — The airport is far away.
Menen lentokentälle taksilla. — I'm going to the airport by taxi.

items.notes: Pronounced 'RAU-ta-tie-A-se-ma'. Compound: rautatie (railway, literally 'iron road') + asema (station). A long but logical word.
examples: Rautatieasema on keskustassa. — The train station is in the center.
Tapaamme rautatieasemalla. — We meet at the train station.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SA-ta-ma'. Partitive: satamaa. Helsinki has a famous harbor area.
examples: Satamassa on laivoja. — There are ships in the harbor.
Kävelen satamaan. — I walk to the harbor.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HO-tel-li'. Double 'l' is long. A loanword, easy to remember. Partitive: hotellia.
examples: Hotelli on kallis. — The hotel is expensive.
Yövymme hotellissa. — We stay at a hotel.

items.notes: Pronounced 'RA-vin-to-la'. From 'ravinto' (nourishment) + -la (place suffix). Partitive: ravintolaa.
examples: Ravintola on täynnä. — The restaurant is full.
Syömme ravintolassa. — We eat at a restaurant.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SAI-raa-la'. From 'sairas' (sick) + -la (place suffix). Double 'a' is long. Partitive: sairaalaa.
examples: Sairaala on iso. — The hospital is big.
Menen sairaalaan. — I'm going to the hospital.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KAUP-pa-kes-kus'. Compound: kauppa (shop) + keskus (center). Double 'p' is long. Partitive: kauppakeskusta.
examples: Kauppakeskus on auki kymmenestä yhdeksään. — The shopping center is open from ten to nine.
Menemme kauppakeskukseen. — We're going to the shopping center.
